question: Are there Consequential Damages on the Project?
raw_answer: The Contractor and the Owner waive claims against each other for consequential damages arising out of or relating to this Agreement.

question: What are the are the Consequential Damages Terms & Conditions?
value: Except as hereinafter provided, the Contractor and the Owner waive claims against each other for consequential damages arising out of or relating to this Agreement. This waiver includes damages incurred by the Contractor for principal office expenses including the compensation of personnel stationed there, for losses of financing, business and reputation, and for loss of profit except anticipated profit arising directly from the Work. This waiver shall in no way apply to or impair the Owner’s right to Liquidated Damages as provided in Section 4.9, nor shall it preclude the Owner from recovering consequential damages with respect to bodily injury or property damage covered by the Contractor’s insurance or that would have been so covered if the Contractor had obtained the insurance required by this Agreement or with respect to bodily injury or property damage claims of 3rd parties for which Contractor provides indemnification pursuant to this Agreement.
raw_answer: The Contractor and the Owner waive claims against each other for consequential damages arising out of or relating to this Agreement, with specific exceptions.

question: Is there a mutual waiver of Consequential Damages in the Contract?
raw_answer: The Contractor and the Owner waive claims against each other for consequential damages arising out of or relating to this Agreement.

question: Are there Liquidated Damages on the Project?
raw_answer: Yes, there are liquidated damages on the project.

question: What are the are the Liquidated Damages Terms & Conditions?
value: Contractor acknowledges that Owner will incur damages, including increased financing costs and loss of revenue caused by delayed occupancy, if Contractor does not timely achieve Substantial Completion of the Project. Contractor further acknowledges that the precise amount of such damages may be incapable of computation. Therefore, Contractor (and its Surety, if any) agrees to pay to Owner as liquidated damages, and not as a penalty, the sum of Two thousand Dollars ($2,000.00) per day, or fraction thereof, for each day that completion is delayed beyond a cure period of 14 business days from the date of Substantial Completion established in accordance with Article 4.1.1. The liquidated damages will be capped at 50% of the Contractor’s fee. Owner shall be entitled to withhold, from any sums otherwise then or thereafter due and payable hereunder, payment of such sums sufficient to offset Contractor's liability for liquidated damages, equal to the amount of any actual delay to the Substantial Completion of the Project. The provisions of this paragraph shall survive the termination of this Agreement or of Contractor's services hereunder.
raw_answer: Contractor agrees to pay $2,000 per day for delays beyond a 14-day cure period, capped at 50% of the Contractor’s fee.

raw_answer: Article 4.9

question: Is a Payment & Performance Bond Required for this project?
raw_answer: Yes, a Payment & Performance Bond is required for this project.

question: What is the Payment & Performance Bond Article #?
What are the Payment & Performance Bond Article Terms & Conditions?
value: The Payment & Performance Bond Article Terms & Conditions include the following key points: 1. The Principal and Surety are held and firmly bound unto the Obligee for the payment of a specified sum. 2. The Principal must perform all undertakings, covenants, terms, conditions, and provisions of the Subcontract within the time provided and any extensions granted. 3. The Surety's obligation is not impaired by any changes, extensions of time, alterations, additions, omissions, or other modifications to the Subcontract. 4. The Surety agrees to perform and complete the work if the Principal fails to do so. 5. The Bond is solely for the protection of claimants supplying labor or materials in the prosecution of the work. 6. Claimants who have not been paid within 90 days after performing the last of the work or furnishing the last of the materials may bring an action on the Bond. 7. No suit or action shall be commenced by any claimant after one year following the date on which the Principal ceased work on the Subcontract. 8. The amount of the Bond is reduced by any payments made in good faith by the Surety.
raw_answer: Payment & Performance Bond terms include obligations of Principal and Surety, protection for claimants, and conditions for legal action.

question: What is the Contract Documents Definition & Incorporation Article #?
What are the Contract Documents Definition & Incorporation Definitions, Terms, & Conditions?
value: The term 'Contract Documents' refers to the documents that form the Contract for Construction. This Contract represents the entire and integrated agreement between the parties regarding the subject matter and supersedes all prior negotiations, representations, or agreements, either written or oral. The Contract may be amended or modified only by a written agreement signed by both parties. The Contract Documents shall not be construed to create a contractual relationship of any kind between the Architect and Contractor, between the Owner and a Subcontractor or Sub-subcontractor, between the Owner and Architect, or between any persons or entities other than the Owner and Contractor. The Owner shall have such rights as a third-party beneficiary of the agreements between the Contractor and its Subcontractors as are specified in this Owner-Contractor Agreement.
raw_answer: The term 'Contract Documents' refers to the documents that form the Contract for Construction.

question: What is the IMC Design Document Review obligations Article #?
What are the Terms & Conditions / IMC's obligations for Design Document Review?
value: The Contractor is obligated to review the Contract Documents and ensure they are adequate for performing the Work and preparing the Guaranteed Maximum Price. The Contractor must participate in bi-weekly Project design meetings and other meetings as required for Budget analysis and value engineering. The Contractor is responsible for taking field measurements, verifying field conditions, and comparing them with the Contract Documents before commencing activities. The Contractor must promptly report any errors, inconsistencies, or omissions discovered to the Owner and Architect. The Contractor is also responsible for maintaining a record copy of the Drawings, Specifications, addenda, Change Orders, and other Modifications at the Site, and providing marked-up drawings showing as-built conditions to the Architect for incorporation into the final design documents. Additionally, the Contractor must review, approve, and submit Shop Drawings, Product Data, Samples, and similar submittals to the Architect, ensuring they conform to the Contract Documents. The Contractor is not relieved of responsibility for deviations from the Contract Documents unless specifically approved in writing by the Architect and Owner.
raw_answer: The Contractor must review Contract Documents, participate in design meetings, take field measurements, report errors, maintain records, and submit Shop Drawings.

question: What is the Submittal Alternates, Substitutions, & Contract Document Deviations Article #?
What are the Submittal Alternates, Substitutions, & Contract Document Deviations Terms & Conditions?
value: Submittal Alternates, Substitutions, & Contract Document Deviations Terms & Conditions are as follows: Contractor shall maintain at the Site for Owner one record copy of the Drawings, Specifications, addenda, Change Orders and other Modifications, in good order and marked currently to record changes and selections made during construction. Shop Drawings, Product Data, Samples and similar submittals are not Contract Documents. Contractor shall review, approve and submit to Architect Shop Drawings, Product Data, Samples and similar submittals. Contractor shall not be relieved of responsibility for deviations from requirements of the Contract Documents by Architect’s approval of Shop Drawings, Product Data, Samples or similar submittals unless Contractor has specifically informed Architect and Owner in writing of such deviation at the time of submittal and Architect and Owner have given written approval to the specific deviation. Contractor shall direct specific attention, in writing or on resubmitted Shop Drawings, Product Data, Samples or similar submittals, to revisions other than those requested by Architect on previous submittals. If Owner prefers to accept Work, which is not in accordance with the requirements of the Contract Documents, Owner may do so instead of requiring its removal and correction, in which case the Contract Sum will be reduced as appropriate and equitable. For additional work to be performed by the Contractor’s Subcontractors, a lump sum itemized to show the net amount of each Subcontractor’s direct costs for labor, materials, equipment rental, and transportation plus a maximum of fifteen percent (15%) as Subcontractor Fee to cover all other charges. For additional work to be performed by the Contractor’s own forces, a lump sum proposal itemized to show the net amount of the Contractor’s direct costs for labor, materials, equipment rental and transportation. For deleted work to be performed by the Contractor’s Subcontractors, the Owner shall receive a lump sum credit equal to the net credit to the Contractor as itemized by his Subcontractors for labor, materials, equipment, transportation and taxes, plus the percentage fee thereof as defined in the agreement. For deleted work to be performed by the Contractor’s own forces, the Owner shall receive a lump-sum credit equal to the net estimated savings to the Contractor based upon Contractor’s original estimate on account of the deleted work for labor, material, equipment, transportation and taxes.
raw_answer: Submittal Alternates, Substitutions, & Contract Document Deviations Terms & Conditions are detailed in the contract documents.

question: What is the GMP Shared Savings Article #?
What are the GMP Shared Savings Terms & Conditions?
value: The GMP Shared Savings Terms & Conditions include the following key points: 1. The GMP will contain Allowance items, and contract terms will not be modified if Allowance Scope is not modified. 2. Changes to the Project are common, and if the GMP is increased or decreased, the General Conditions, Insurance, Bond, and Fee will be adjusted within the parameters established by the Owner/Contractor Agreement. 3. Subcontract Change Orders must be submitted to the Owner for review and written approval before the Contractor issues them to the Subcontractor. 4. All Subcontract Change Orders must be countersigned by the subcontractor before requesting payment for change order work. 5. All contracts, change orders, and GMP proposals must be segregated by Project Elements or any other reasonable component of the Project. 6. The Contractor must provide the Owner with a Monthly Project Report, including a Budget/Cost Report, project schedule status, a 60-day short-term look-ahead schedule, a schedule narrative, direct work subcontractor contracts and change order status, a critical issue narrative, project cash flow projections, and progress photos. 7. All Budgets/Cost Reports and GMP proposals must be segregated by Project Elements or any other reasonable component of the Project. 8. The Contractor must establish a cost control system (Field Work Authorization) to notify the Owner of cost events and potential schedule impacts. 9. The Contractor must not proceed with any extra work within the GMP or beyond the GMP without written authorization. 10. Post-construction services include compiling and delivering required submittals, coordinating systems testing, commissioning and turnover, and issuing a final request for subcontractor payment.
raw_answer: The GMP Shared Savings Terms & Conditions include provisions for allowance items, change orders, monthly project reports, cost control systems, and post-construction services.

question: What are the GMP Buyout Savings Definition & Terms & Conditions?
value: The GMP Buyout Savings Definition & Terms & Conditions are outlined in various sections of the contract documents. The GMP (Guaranteed Maximum Price) includes the estimated Cost of Work and Contractor’s Fee, and it is subject to adjustment as provided in the agreement. Changes to the project may result in adjustments to the General Conditions, Insurance, Bond, and Fee within the parameters established by the Owner/Contractor Agreement. Subcontract Change Orders must be reviewed and approved by the Owner before issuance. The Contractor is required to provide monthly project reports, including budget/cost reports, project schedule status, and cash flow projections. All contracts, change orders, and GMP proposals must be segregated by project elements or other reasonable components. The Contract Sum, including authorized adjustments, is the total amount payable by the Owner to the Contractor and shall not exceed the GMP, subject to additions and deductions by Change Order. Costs exceeding the GMP shall be paid by the Contractor without reimbursement by the Owner. The Owner may withhold consent to the GMP if it is not acceptable and may terminate the agreement or the Contractor's right to proceed with the work. The Contractor must establish a cost control system to notify the Owner of potential changes to the work and must not proceed with extra work without written authorization.
raw_answer: The GMP Buyout Savings Definition & Terms & Conditions are outlined in various sections of the contract documents.

question: What is the Contingency Usage Article #?
What are the Contingency Usage Terms and Conditions?
value: The Contingency Usage Terms and Conditions are outlined in the Owner-Contractor Agreement. Specifically, the Contractor's reasonable and necessary costs and expenses incurred in additional work due to delays may be included in the Cost of the Work and charged to the Contingency, if any, but they shall not cause any increase in the Guaranteed Maximum Price. Additionally, the Owner has the right to direct the Contractor to perform overtime work or shift work to return to the original Construction Schedule, with the costs included in the Cost of the Work and the Guaranteed Maximum Price increased accordingly.
raw_answer: Contingency costs may be included in the Cost of the Work but shall not increase the Guaranteed Maximum Price. Owner can direct overtime work to return to the original schedule, with costs included in the Cost of the Work.

question: How many Adverse Weather Days Included in Contract Agreement?
What is the Schedule Requirements Article #?
What are the Schedule Requirements Terms & Conditions?
value: The schedule requirements terms and conditions include the following: The Contractor is responsible for issuing a Construction Schedule consistent with the scheduling requirements of all Project Elements, incorporating activity sequence and durations agreed upon with subcontractors and vendors. The schedule must be in the form of a CPM arrow network diagram, organized by numerically identified activities, and include various elements such as subcontractor procurement process, shop drawing and submittal schedule, planned progressions of construction work, and appropriate restraints. The Construction Schedule must be submitted to the Owner for approval and, once approved, becomes part of the Contract Documents. The schedule must be updated regularly, and any changes must be approved by the Owner. The Baseline Schedule, included in the approved GMP contract amendment, is used for comparison and tracking with future updates. The Contractor must achieve Substantial Completion by the time established in the Construction Schedule and Final Completion within ninety days after Substantial Completion. Time is of the essence, and delays due to Force Majeure events may extend the Time of Completion. The Contractor must also provide a computerized mathematical analysis of the CPM logic with each monthly schedule update.

The schedule must be in the form of a CPM arrow network diagram, organized by numerically identified activities, and include various elements such as subcontractor procurement process, shop drawing and submittal schedule, planned progressions of construction work, and appropriate restraints. The Construction Schedule must be submitted to the Owner for approval and, once approved, becomes part of the Contract Documents. question: What is the Daily Log & Manpower Article #?
raw_answer: Article 4

question: What are the Daily Log & Manpower Terms and Conditions?
value: The Daily Log & Manpower Terms and Conditions include the following: The Contractor is required to provide the Owner with a Monthly Project Report, which must include a latest monthly estimate summary or a Project Budget/Cost Report, overall project schedule status, a 60-day short term look ahead schedule, a schedule narrative, all direct work subcontractor’s contracts and change order status, a critical issue narrative, project cash flow projections, and progress photos. All Budgets/Cost Reports and GMP proposals must be segregated by Project Elements or any other reasonable component of the Project. The Contractor must submit the monthly Project Report with the Contractor’s monthly Application for Payment, and the Application for Payment will not be reviewed and approved until the monthly Project Report is submitted to the Owner and Project Manager. The Contractor must provide an electronic copy and two copies to the Owner and Project Manager. Payment for stored materials is subject to Owner's approval and must follow the procedures required by the Owner. Equipment and certain major elements of the work will be paid if properly stored, if the vendor's invoice is received, an insurance certificate for the value of the stored material is provided, and title to the material has passed to the Owner. Payment will not be made for common materials brought to the site in bulk. Prepayment accounts for General Conditions are not allowed, and only actual, incurred costs will be billed for the month. The Contractor must provide actual invoices, bills, and receipts to support the General Conditions.
raw_answer: The Contractor must provide a Monthly Project Report with various details and follow specific procedures for payment and storage of materials.

question: What is the Schedule Delay / Time Issues Article #?
What are the Schedule Delay / Time Issues Terms & Conditions?
value: The Schedule Delay / Time Issues Terms & Conditions include several key points: 1. The Contractor must meet critical path dates and, if failing to do so, must work overtime or shift work to return to the original schedule without any adjustment to the Guaranteed Maximum Price. 2. The Construction Schedule must be regularly updated but cannot extend the Time of Completion or milestone dates without a written Change Order from the Owner. 3. Time is of the essence in the performance of the Contract. 4. Delays caused by Force Majeure events (e.g., fire, extreme weather, war) can extend the Time of Completion if the Contractor requests an extension in writing within ten days of the delay. 5. The Contractor must demonstrate that the delay impacted the critical path of the Construction Schedule. 6. The Owner can direct the Contractor to work additional hours to mitigate delays, with costs included in the Cost of the Work and the Guaranteed Maximum Price adjusted accordingly. 7. Delays caused by the Owner or concurrent delays will result in a time extension and shared costs. 8. The Contractor is responsible for maintaining the Construction Schedule and meeting all critical dates. 9. Liquidated damages may apply for delays in achieving Substantial Completion. 10. The Contractor's sole remedy for delays is an extension of time and reimbursement of proven costs, with no entitlement to other damages.
raw_answer: The Schedule Delay / Time Issues Terms & Conditions include provisions for critical path dates, regular updates to the Construction Schedule, Force Majeure events, Owner-directed overtime work, concurrent delays, and liquidated damages for delays.

question: How much Schedule Delay / Time Issues Notice to Owner Requirements (Within What # of Days)?
raw_answer: ten (10) days

question: What is the Schedule Acceleration Directed by Owner Article #?
What are the Schedule Acceleration Directed by Owner Terms & Conditions?
value: If the Contractor is delayed in the performance of Work on the critical path due to a Force Majeure Event, the Owner has the right to direct the Contractor to perform overtime or shift work to return to the original Construction Schedule. The Contractor's reasonable and necessary costs incurred in such additional work will be included in the Cost of the Work, and the Guaranteed Maximum Price (GMP) will be increased accordingly. If a Force Majeure Event justifies an extension of the Time of Completion and/or milestone dates, the parties will execute a Change Order to adjust the Contract Time and Contract Sum. The Contractor must prepare a mutually agreeable schedule recovery plan if they fail to comply with the Construction Schedule or meet any Project milestone dates. The Contractor will bear the costs of additional work to return to the original Construction Schedule, which may be funded from the Construction Contingency, but no adjustment will be made to the GMP. The Owner may terminate the agreement if the Contractor's proposed Construction Schedule is not acceptable. Time is of the essence in the performance of the Contract.
raw_answer: Owner can direct overtime work to return to schedule; costs included in Work Cost and GMP adjusted. Extensions and Change Orders for Force Majeure. Contractor must prepare recovery plan for delays; costs borne by Contractor, funded from Contingency, no GMP adjustment. Owner may terminate if schedule unacceptable. Time is of the essence.

question: What are the Schedule Definitions of Days (Work / Calendar / Holidays / Etc.)?
value: Contractor and its employees, Subcontractors, and all other parties covered by the Contract are required to work at hours and on days, in addition to the normal working hours and normal working days, including overtime work or shift work as necessary to meet the Construction Schedule. The Owner may direct such additional work in writing, especially in cases of delays due to Force Majeure events.
raw_answer: Contractor and its employees, Subcontractors, and all other parties covered by the Contract are required to work at hours and on days, in addition to the normal working hours and normal working days, including overtime work or shift work as necessary to meet the Construction Schedule.

question: What is the Substantial Completion Article #?
What are the Substantial Completion Terms & Conditions?
value: Substantial Completion is the date proposed by the Contractor, certified by the Architect, and approved by the Owner when the Work or a designated portion thereof meets specific conditions. These conditions include completion in accordance with the Contract Documents (except for minor punch list items), full operational status of elevators, heating, plumbing, ventilating, air conditioning, and life safety systems, suitability for intended use, issuance of a Certificate of Substantial Completion by the Architect, issuance of a final or temporary Certificate of Occupancy by the relevant authorities, and submission of the Certificate of Substantial Completion to the Owner and Contractor for written acceptance. Upon acceptance and consent of surety, the Owner shall make payment of retainage less 150% of the cost to complete the remaining work. The Contractor must complete all punch list items within 30 days and achieve Final Completion within 90 days after Substantial Completion.
raw_answer: Substantial Completion is the date proposed by the Contractor, certified by the Architect, and approved by the Owner when the Work or a designated portion thereof meets specific conditions.

question: What is the Final Completion Definition Article #?
What is the Final Completion Definition?
value: The 'Final Completion' of the Work is that date on which, according to a Certification of the Architect, Contractor has completed all the Work, including but not limited to satisfactory operation of all equipment, by means of acceptance tests, correction of all punch list items to the satisfaction of Architect, release of all mechanics', materialmen's, construction and like liens, delivery of all guarantees, three copies of all equipment operation and maintenance manuals, as-built drawings, final or Temporary Certificates of Occupancy, or other certificates required for occupancy, mechanical, electrical and plumbing certificates, all other required approvals and acceptances by city, county and state governments, or other authorities having jurisdiction, and removal of all rubbish, tools, scaffolding and surplus materials and equipment from the Project Site.
raw_answer: The 'Final Completion' of the Work is that date on which, according to a Certification of the Architect, Contractor has completed all the Work, including but not limited to satisfactory operation of all equipment, by means of acceptance tests, correction of all punch list items to the satisfaction of Architect, release of all mechanics', materialmen's, construction and like liens, delivery of all guarantees, three copies of all equipment operation and maintenance manuals, as-built drawings, final or Temporary Certificates of Occupancy, or other certificates required for occupancy, mechanical, electrical and plumbing certificates, all other required approvals and acceptances by city, county and state governments, or other authorities having jurisdiction, and removal of all rubbish, tools, scaffolding and surplus materials and equipment from the Project Site.

value: EXHIBIT A

question: What are the Warranty Terms & Conditions?
value: The Contractor's Warranty Certificate includes the following terms and conditions: 1. All material and equipment furnished under the Agreement is new, of first-class quality, free from faults and defects, and in conformance with the Contract Documents. Any non-conforming work is considered defective. 2. The Contractor must promptly correct all defective work rejected by the Owner or Architect, bearing all costs of correction. 3. If any work or equipment is found defective within one year after Substantial Completion, or within a longer period specified in Exhibit A or the Agreement, the Contractor must correct it promptly after receiving written notice from the Owner and Architect. 4. Subcontractors employed by the Contractor have warranted their portion of the work for the Warranty Period, benefiting the Owner, Lender, and Contractor. The Contractor must make good all defects and damage caused by such defects at its own cost during the Warranty Period. 5. The Contractor has obtained guarantees from material and equipment suppliers for the Warranty Period, benefiting the Owner and Contractor. 6. The warranty benefits the Owner, Lender, and Contractor and their successors or assigns and may be enforced by each of them. The warranty does not modify or limit any obligations, liabilities, and duties of the Contractor under the Agreement. The warranty remains binding and irrevocable during the stated periods, and the Contractor cannot contest its validity or withdraw from it for any cause.
raw_answer: The Contractor's Warranty Certificate includes terms ensuring new, first-class quality materials and equipment, prompt correction of defects, and guarantees from subcontractors and suppliers, benefiting the Owner, Lender, and Contractor.

question: What is the As-Built / Record Documents Requirements Article #?
value: 1.a.i
raw_answer: 1.a.i

question: What are the As-Built / Record Documents Requirements?
value: Contractor shall maintain at the Site for Owner one record copy of the Drawings, Specifications, addenda, Change Orders and other Modifications, in good order and marked currently to record changes and selections made during construction, and in addition approved Shop Drawings, Product Data, Samples and similar required submittals. The marked up drawings showing as-built conditions shall be provided to the Architect for incorporation into the design documents as the final record set of design drawings. All of the above information shall be delivered to Owner in good condition upon completion of the Work and before final payment is made, and shall be signed by Contractor, certifying that they show complete and exact as-built conditions, stating sizes, kind of materials, vital piping, conduit locations and similar matters. Contractor shall maintain all approved permit drawings in a manner so as to make them accessible to governmental inspectors and other authorized agencies. All approved drawings shall be wrapped, marked and delivered to Owner within sixty (60) days of final completion of the Work.
raw_answer: Contractor shall maintain at the Site for Owner one record copy of the Drawings, Specifications, addenda, Change Orders and other Modifications, in good order and marked currently to record changes and selections made during construction, and in addition approved Shop Drawings, Product Data, Samples and similar required submittals. The marked up drawings showing as-built conditions shall be provided to the Architect for incorporation into the design documents as the final record set of design drawings. All of the above information shall be delivered to Owner in good condition upon completion of the Work and before final payment is made, and shall be signed by Contractor, certifying that they show complete and exact as-built conditions, stating sizes, kind of materials, vital piping, conduit locations and similar matters. Contractor shall maintain all approved permit drawings in a manner so as to make them accessible to governmental inspectors and other authorized agencies. All approved drawings shall be wrapped, marked and delivered to Owner within sixty (60) days of final completion of the Work.

question: What is the Final Payment Article #?
What are the Final Payment Terms & Conditions?
value: The final payment terms and conditions include the following: All applications for payment must include certified subcontractor payment requests and signed waivers of liens for the work performed under the billing period. Payment for stored materials is subject to the owner's approval and must follow specific procedures. The final payment request must include a subcontractor’s final contract value summary, warranty letter, final release of liens, consent of surety (if applicable), a statement that all punchlist work is complete, and a copy of all close-out documents in digital form. The contractor must also submit a final payment certification and waiver of claims and liens, certifying that all amounts due under the contract have been paid in full and waiving any further claims against the owner. Additionally, the contractor must achieve final completion within ninety days after substantial completion, and the final completion must be confirmed in writing by the owner and contractor.
raw_answer: Final payment terms and conditions include certified subcontractor payment requests, signed waivers of liens, owner's approval for stored materials, final contract value summary, warranty letter, final release of liens, consent of surety, statement of punchlist completion, digital close-out documents, final payment certification and waiver of claims and liens, and written confirmation of final completion within ninety days after substantial completion.

question: What is the Concealed / Unknown / Hazardous Conditions Article #?
What are the Concealed / Unknown / Hazardous Conditions Terms & Conditions?
value: The Owner assumes no responsibility for the accuracy or completeness of documents provided and the Contractor releases the Owner from any claim arising from reliance on such documents. The Contractor and Subcontractors must evaluate and satisfy themselves regarding the conditions and limitations under which the Work is to be performed, including site conditions, climatic conditions, labor supply and costs, availability and cost of materials, tools, and equipment. The Owner assumes no responsibility for the physical conditions or safety of the Project site. The Contractor is solely responsible for providing a safe place for the performance of the Work. The Contract Sum or Contract Time will not be adjusted for any failure by the Contractor or Subcontractors to comply with these requirements. The Contractor must visit the Site, become familiar with local conditions, and rely solely on its own observations and any written representations made by the Owner regarding the Site. The Guaranteed Maximum Price will not be increased due to site conditions unless they differ materially from those represented in the Contract Documents, in which case the Guaranteed Maximum Price and Time of Completion will be adjusted accordingly.

The Owner assumes no responsibility for the physical conditions or safety of the Project site. The Contractor is solely responsible for providing a safe place for the performance of the Work. The Contract Sum or Contract Time will not be adjusted for any failure by the Contractor or Subcontractors to comply with these requirements. The Contractor must visit the Site, become familiar with local conditions, and rely solely on its own observations and any written representations made by the Owner regarding the Site. The Guaranteed Maximum Price will not be increased due to site conditions unless they differ materially from those represented in the Contract Documents, in which case the Guaranteed Maximum Price and Time of Completion will be adjusted accordingly.

question: What is the Labor?
value: Labor refers to the work performed or services provided in the prosecution of the Work under the Subcontract, including public utility services and reasonable rentals of equipment.
